summary: USMCA's review dominates the day. USTR is sending technical negotiators to Mexico as Jamieson Greer pushes to shrink the US trade deficit, while Sheinbaum wants a deal before September. Automakers say a 19% tariff blocks rules-of-origin talks, BlackRock warns annual reviews add investment uncertainty, and Pemex halted Cuba oil exports for the first time in 33 years under US pressure. Ernesto Ruffo's arrest adds to security strains shadowing the negotiations.

lead.h1: USTR delegation to visit Mexico as US pushes to cut trade deficit
lead.context: Jamieson Greer says the US wants to shrink its trade deficit with Mexico as USTR sends technical negotiators ahead of the USMCA review, signaling Washington will use the review to press for structural changes in trade flows.
lead.background: The USMCA review is approaching, and USTR chief Jamieson Greer spoke publicly at the Aspen Security Forum about Washington's goals for that review.
lead.drivers: Greer said he has a mandate from the president to control the trade deficit with Mexico, whether through tariffs, quotas or other mechanisms, and wants tighter rules of origin and more balanced production distribution between the two countries, arguing the deficit reflects subsidies, industrial overcapacity and unfair trade practices.
lead.implications: The review will no longer focus only on modernizing the agreement but on redesigning trade conditions to favor relocating manufacturing to US territory, which could tighten USMCA rules limiting growth of Mexican exports to the United States, particularly in manufacturing sectors.

items.headline: Sheinbaum says USMCA review enters key phase, seeks deal before September
items.context: Her comments set a rough timeline for resolving the review, a process now complicated by US demands for tariffs and quotas on Mexican exports.

items.headline: Former Baja California governor Ernesto Ruffo arrested on organized crime charges
items.context: Prosecutors said the arrest, tied to a fuel-theft investigation into his company, followed a high-complexity probe; a business partner was also detained.

items.headline: Pemex halts oil exports to Cuba for first time in 33 years
items.context: The state oil company stops shipments after Trump administration warnings, a move that signals Mexico's caution in handling energy ties with Havana under US pressure.
items.background: Pemex had exported oil to Cuba continuously since official records began in 1993, and Banxico data show the value of those exports had been falling steadily since January 2026.
items.drivers: The halt comes amid pressure from the Trump administration, which has threatened to intervene to remove Miguel Diaz-Canel from power, even as President Claudia Sheinbaum said at the end of June that Mexico would continue sending oil.

items.headline: BlackRock says annual USMCA reviews will bring uncertainty to Mexico investment
items.context: BlackRock does not expect Mexico to lose its sovereign investment-grade rating, but flags the treaty's annual review mechanism as a recurring source of uncertainty for investors in the country.

items.headline: Finance ministry accelerates cuts to productive investment to control fiscal deficit
items.context: Hacienda is cutting spending on productive investment ahead of the second half of the year in an effort to contain the fiscal deficit, according to the report.
items.background: Public finance data from the Finance Ministry through the end of May show a fiscal consolidation strategy centered on cutting productive investment, even as the SAT has increased revenue collection this year.
items.drivers: Hacienda, headed by Edgar Amador Zamora, has relied on Pemex investment cuts to ease pressure on the fiscal framework, while spending on social development continues to rise.
items.implications: Physical investment in Pemex fell to 77,188 million pesos in the first five months of the year, its lowest monetary value since 2008, with capital for new machinery down 45.7% in real terms year over year, its largest historical drop for a similar period, and a 25.5% contraction in May alone.

items.headline: Rocha Moya case and organized crime complicate USMCA outlook
items.context: The decision to hold annual reviews instead of extending the deal for 16 years is read as political pressure from the Trump administration tied to corruption and cartel cases implicating Mexican officials.
items.background: Analyst David Saucedo made the remarks in an interview with EL CEO, tying the shift to annual reviews rather than a 16-year extension to the case of former Sinaloa governor Rubén Rocha Moya. He said the move could be seen as retaliation by the Trump administration over the lack of action against Rocha Moya. Saucedo added that the US stance also reflects broader factors including counternarcotics cooperation and Washington's view of how the Mexican government has handled organized crime.

items.headline: Auto industry says sectoral tariffs block rules-of-origin talks with US
items.context: The head of Mexico's auto industry association says automakers paying a 19% tariff to Washington cannot negotiate rules of origin while sectoral tariffs remain in place, tying the USMCA review directly to the sector's cost structure.

standing.text: The peso trades at 17.44 pesos to the dollar; inflation is 3.37%; the policy rate is 6.50%.

live.tmpl: the peso trades at {v} pesos to the dollar
